displayName: pixi.js (v7) 2D WebGL Game and Toy Guidelines
description: Quality and correctness rules for offline single-file pixi.js (v7) WebGL 2D mini-apps (bullet-hell shooters, fluid blobs, shader-filter kaleidoscopes, GPU particle fountains, neon breakout, sprite stress arenas, aquariums, cursor-trail toys). Covers the classic UMD script-tag load contract (no ESM under file://), pinning v7 (v8 changed the API), devicePixelRatio clamping, ParticleContainer and sprite pooling for thousands of sprites, the ticker for delta-time motion, destroying textures on teardown, full-viewport responsive stages, and shipping a complete interactive experience. For pixi.js 2D mini-apps only; do not leak into three.js 3D games or productivity apps.

guidelines.rule: Load pixi.js with a classic UMD <script src> tag that exposes the global PIXI. NEVER use an ESM 'import * as PIXI from "pixi.js"' / <script type=module>. ESM modules silently fail to load under file:// in the WKWebView preview, leaving a blank stage.
guidelines.why: These mini-apps ship as static single-file bundles and run under file:// inside WKWebView, which is not a secure context. A bare module import or an importmap referencing pixi.js does not resolve there and PIXI is never defined. The UMD build attaches PIXI to window, which works offline under file://.
examples: GOOD: <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/pixi.js@7.4.2/dist/pixi.min.js"></script> then new PIXI.Application(...)
WRONG: import * as PIXI from 'pixi.js'; - ESM does not resolve under file:// in the preview
WRONG: <script type="module"> import { Application } from 'pixi.js'; </script> - blank stage

guidelines.rule: Pin pixi.js v7 specifically (for example pixi.js@7.4.2). v8 changed the API substantially (async Application.init(), renamed members, ParticleContainer moved). Code against v7's synchronous 'new PIXI.Application({...})' API and pin a v7 URL so the vendored copy matches.
guidelines.why: pixi v8 is a breaking redesign: Application setup became async (await app.init), several classes and options were renamed/removed. Code written for v7 silently breaks on v8 and vice versa. Pinning a v7 version keeps the bundle deterministic and the synchronous constructor working.
examples: GOOD: <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/pixi.js@7.4.2/dist/pixi.min.js"></script>
GOOD (v7 API): const app = new PIXI.Application({ resizeTo: window, antialias: true, background: 0x05060f });
WRONG: pinning pixi.js@8 then using the synchronous v7 constructor

guidelines.rule: Clamp the renderer resolution. Pass resolution: Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio || 1, 2) and autoDensity: true to the Application, and re-apply on resize. Never render at the raw resolution of a 3x Retina phone.
guidelines.why: A 3x DPR phone renders 9x the pixels; on a sprite/particle-heavy WebGL scene that is the difference between 60fps and a slideshow. Clamping resolution to 2 with autoDensity keeps the image crisp while capping the pixel budget. This is the single highest-ROI mobile performance fix.
examples: new PIXI.Application({ resolution: Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio||1, 2), autoDensity: true, resizeTo: window });
WRONG: resolution: window.devicePixelRatio with no clamp

guidelines.rule: For thousands of sprites (bullet-hell, particle fountains, sprite arenas), use a PIXI.ParticleContainer and a SHARED texture, and POOL the sprites: pre-allocate a fixed array of sprites and recycle them (hide/show, reposition) instead of creating and destroying sprites every frame or every shot. Keep all instances on one base texture.
guidelines.why: ParticleContainer batches huge numbers of same-texture sprites into very few draw calls; a normal Container of thousands of distinct sprites is slow. Allocating a Sprite per bullet/particle every frame triggers GC churn and the destroy cost of pixi-007. A fixed pool keeps allocation flat: spawning takes a dead slot, despawning hides it.
examples: const pc = new PIXI.ParticleContainer(20000, { position: true, scale: true, alpha: true });
pool: pre-make sprites from one texture; spawn() = take a hidden one and reposition; despawn() = sprite.visible=false
WRONG: new PIXI.Sprite(tex) inside the per-frame loop for every bullet

guidelines.rule: Drive all motion from the ticker with delta time. Use app.ticker.add((delta) => ...) and scale movement by delta (or by ticker.deltaMS/1000 for seconds), so motion is identical at 30/60/120fps. Clamp very large deltas after a stall. Do NOT advance by a constant amount per tick.
guidelines.why: pixi's ticker runs at the display refresh, which varies by device and stalls when backgrounded. Per-tick constant motion makes a 120Hz phone run the game twice as fast as a 60Hz one. Scaling by the ticker delta gives deterministic motion everywhere; clamping the delta avoids a teleport after a tab stall.
examples: app.ticker.add((delta) => { const dt = Math.min(delta, 3); update(dt); });
or: const dt = Math.min(app.ticker.deltaMS/1000, 0.1); pos.x += vx*dt;
WRONG: sprite.x += speed; // runs faster on high-refresh displays

guidelines.rule: Generate sprite textures procedurally with PIXI.Graphics + app.renderer.generateTexture (or render a Graphics directly) - do NOT fetch or embed image asset files. Draw a glow circle, a ship, a brick, a fish as Graphics once, bake it to a texture, then reuse that texture across all sprites.
guidelines.why: Image files mean network fetches (dead under file://) or large base64 blobs that bloat the bundle. A Graphics-drawn shape baked to a texture once gives crisp, reusable sprites with zero bytes and zero network, fully offline. Generating one texture and sharing it also enables ParticleContainer batching (pixi-004).
examples: const g = new PIXI.Graphics().beginFill(0x00e5ff).drawCircle(0,0,8).endFill(); const tex = app.renderer.generateTexture(g);
reuse tex for every particle/bullet sprite
WRONG: PIXI.Sprite.from('ship.png') or loading an image URL - breaks offline

guidelines.rule: Destroy GPU resources on teardown. Call app.destroy(true, { children: true, texture: true, baseTexture: true }) (or destroy your generated textures explicitly) when the mini-app ends or the view tears down, and remove the resize/pointer listeners. Generated textures and render targets are not garbage-collected on their own.
guidelines.why: pixi keeps generated textures, render targets, and filters in WebGL memory until destroyed; a toy that rebuilds its stage on restart without destroying leaks VRAM until the context is lost and the canvas goes black. Mini-apps get torn down and relaunched repeatedly in the host, so the leak compounds fast.
examples: function teardown(){ window.removeEventListener('resize', onResize); app.destroy(true, { children:true, texture:true, baseTexture:true }); }
explicitly: generatedTexture.destroy(true); filter.destroy?.();

guidelines.rule: Make the stage fill and resize to the viewport. Pass resizeTo: window (or resize the renderer on the resize/orientationchange events) and reposition the gameplay layout (paddle bounds, fountain origin, fish bounds) to the new size. Set the canvas display:block and the body margin to 0 so there are no gaps.
guidelines.why: A fixed-size stage leaves dead margins and looks unfinished, and the preview/hub render at many sizes. resizeTo:window keeps the canvas edge-to-edge; recomputing layout bounds on resize keeps gameplay correct after rotation. Without margin:0 / display:block the canvas inherits the default body margin.
examples: new PIXI.Application({ resizeTo: window, ... }); window.addEventListener('resize', repositionLayout);
CSS: html,body{margin:0;overflow:hidden} canvas{display:block}
on resize, recompute paddle/wall/fountain positions from app.screen.width/height

guidelines.rule: Use pixi's filters for shader-style effects rather than hand-writing GLSL: DisplacementFilter for warp/fluid, ColorMatrixFilter for color shifts, BlurFilter for glow, AlphaFilter, etc. Apply them to a container's filters array, and dispose them on teardown. For neon glow, blend additively (sprite.blendMode = PIXI.BLEND_MODES.ADD).
guidelines.why: Pixi's built-in filters give kaleidoscope, displacement, and color effects with no custom shader code and they survive offline file://. Additive blend mode is how particle fountains and cursor trails get their luminous look. Filters hold GPU resources, so include them in the teardown disposal.
examples: container.filters = [ new PIXI.DisplacementFilter(displacementSprite), new PIXI.ColorMatrixFilter() ];
glow: sprite.blendMode = PIXI.BLEND_MODES.ADD;
dispose filters in teardown along with their displacement textures

guidelines.rule: Support BOTH pointer and touch via pixi's unified pointer events and/or DOM pointer events. Use 'pointermove'/'pointerdown'/'pointerup' (which cover mouse and touch) on app.stage with stage.eventMode='static' and stage.hitArea = app.screen, or listen on the canvas. Set CSS touch-action:none on the canvas. Never rely on hover-only interaction.
guidelines.why: These toys are tried on phones; mouse-only handlers leave a pretty but dead app. pixi's pointer events are unified across mouse and touch. touch-action:none stops the browser from hijacking drags for scroll/zoom so dragging controls the paddle/blob/fountain instead of panning the page.
examples: app.stage.eventMode = 'static'; app.stage.hitArea = app.screen; app.stage.on('pointermove', e => aimAt(e.global));
CSS: canvas{touch-action:none;display:block}
WRONG: a mousemove-only paddle that does nothing on a phone

guidelines.rule: Cap the active entity budget and scale it down on mobile/small screens. Bound the number of live bullets/particles/sprites, lower the cap when the screen is small or the device pixel ratio is high, and reuse from the pool (pixi-004). Watch that you do not spawn unbounded entities until the frame rate collapses.
guidelines.why: Bullet-hell and particle toys love to spawn unbounded entities until mobile chokes. A bounded, pooled budget keeps the frame rate steady; scaling the cap to screen size keeps a phone smooth while a desktop can show more. This works together with the resolution clamp (pixi-003).
examples: const MAX_PARTICLES = (window.innerWidth < 700) ? 4000 : 12000;
if (liveCount >= MAX_PARTICLES) reuseOldest(); else spawnFromPool();
WRONG: emitting particles every frame with no upper bound

guidelines.rule: Ship a complete, beautiful, interactive experience on the first frame - never a blank or single-static stage. Seed the scene (fish swimming, particles flowing, bricks laid out, enemies spawning) so it is alive before any input, and wire interaction so the first gesture has an immediate visible effect. For GAME goals, ship a real playable loop with score and a reachable win/lose state, not just an ambient scene.
guidelines.why: The render gate and taste rubric can pass a blank-but-loaded stage. The product is a living, responsive toy or a real game. A fountain with no particles, a breakout with no ball, or an aquarium with no fish reads as broken even though it 'loaded'. A game with no win/lose is a tech demo, not a game.
examples: aquarium: fish already swimming and bubbles rising on load
breakout: ball, paddle, and full brick wall present; lives/score HUD; win on clear, lose on no balls
WRONG: an empty stage that needs a click before anything appears

guidelines.rule: Use the SYNCHRONOUS v7 Application constructor and NEVER the v8 async init. Write `const app = new PIXI.Application({ ...options }); document.getElementById('stage').appendChild(app.view);` and start drawing immediately. Do NOT write `const app = new PIXI.Application(); await app.init({...});` or `PIXI.Application.init(...)` - that is the v8 API and against the pinned v7 build it leaves app.view/app.renderer undefined and the stage never renders. There is no await, no .init(), and no top-level async in a v7 setup.
guidelines.why: The particle-heavy fountain, sprite-swarm, and fluid-blobs builds all hit autoFixGaveUp, and the load-order/init-model drift between v6/v7 (sync constructor) and v8 (async init) is a prime silent failure: with v8-style `await app.init()` against the vendored v7 bundle, the options are never applied, app.view is undefined, appendChild throws or appends nothing, and nothing renders with no obvious error for the autofixer to converge on. Pinning v7 (pixi-002) only helps if the code also uses the v7 sync constructor.
examples: GOOD (v7): const app = new PIXI.Application({ resizeTo: window, antialias: true, background: 0x05060f }); document.body.appendChild(app.view);
WRONG (v8): const app = new PIXI.Application(); await app.init({ resizeTo: window }); - undefined view on the v7 bundle, blank stage
WRONG: wrapping setup in `(async () => { await app.init() ... })()` for a v7 target

guidelines.rule: Render every particle/sprite with app.stage and the app.ticker loop. After constructing the Application, build your scene under app.stage (`app.stage.addChild(particleContainer)`) and drive ALL motion from `app.ticker.add((delta) => { ... })`. Do NOT hand-roll a requestAnimationFrame loop that calls app.render() yourself, and do NOT add display objects to a detached container that is never added to app.stage. A ParticleContainer that is created but never added to app.stage renders nothing - a classic blank-fountain failure.
guidelines.why: The particle fountain/swarm autofix loops failed partly because sprites were created and updated but the container was never parented to app.stage, or motion ran in a separate rAF that fought pixi's own render. pixi already renders app.stage on its internal ticker; a parallel rAF + manual app.render() double-renders or never renders. The fix is one ownership model: scene under app.stage, motion in app.ticker.
examples: const pc = new PIXI.ParticleContainer(40000, { position:true, scale:true, alpha:true, tint:true }); app.stage.addChild(pc);
app.ticker.add((delta) => { for (const p of pool) { if (p.sprite.visible) { p.update(delta); } } });
WRONG: const pc = new PIXI.ParticleContainer(...); /* never app.stage.addChild(pc) */ - renders nothing
WRONG: function loop(){ updateParticles(); app.render(); requestAnimationFrame(loop); } - fights the built-in ticker

guidelines.rule: Declare the ParticleContainer property flags with the EXACT v7 boolean signature for what you animate. Construct it as `new PIXI.ParticleContainer(maxSize, { scale: true, position: true, rotation: false, uvs: false, tint: true, alpha: true })`, and turn ON every property you mutate in the ticker. If you animate a sprite's tint or alpha but the flag is false, the change silently never reaches the GPU and particles look frozen or invisible. Set rotation:true only if you actually spin sprites; set uvs:true only for animated/atlas frames.
guidelines.why: The 3 high-complexity particle apps (fountain, sprite-swarm, fluid-blobs) all hit autoFixGaveUp, and a top cause is ParticleContainer's per-property upload flags: in v7, properties you mutate but did not enable are not re-uploaded each frame, so animating tint/alpha with tint:false/alpha:false produces a static or invisible swarm with no error. Naming the exact six-key signature and tying each flag to what is animated removes the guesswork the autofixer kept failing on.
examples: fountain (sparks fade + shift color): new PIXI.ParticleContainer(50000, { scale:true, position:true, rotation:false, uvs:false, tint:true, alpha:true })
swarm (move + scale only): new PIXI.ParticleContainer(20000, { scale:true, position:true, rotation:false, uvs:false, tint:false, alpha:false })
WRONG: animating sprite.alpha and sprite.tint while ParticleContainer was made with { position:true } only - those mutations never render

guidelines.rule: Build every particle texture procedurally with PIXI.Graphics + app.renderer.generateTexture (or a small offscreen canvas baked via PIXI.Texture.from(canvas)) - NEVER fetch a CDN/URL image or PIXI.Sprite.from('something.png'). Bake ONE shared base texture for the whole swarm before the ticker starts, and reuse it for every pooled sprite so ParticleContainer can batch them into a few draw calls.
guidelines.why: Image-asset loads are dead under file:// (network) and also break ParticleContainer batching when sprites end up on different base textures. The working breakout/aquarium builds bake canvas/Graphics textures and reuse them; the failed particle apps risk per-sprite textures or remote assets. One Graphics-baked shared texture is offline-safe, zero-byte, and batch-friendly - directly preventing the blank/slow fountain failures.
examples: const g = new PIXI.Graphics().beginFill(0xffffff).drawCircle(0,0,16).endFill(); const sparkTex = app.renderer.generateTexture(g); g.destroy();
pool: for (let i=0;i<MAX;i++){ const s = new PIXI.Sprite(sparkTex); s.visible=false; s.tint=0xffcc66; pc.addChild(s); pool.push(s); }
WRONG: PIXI.Sprite.from('https://.../spark.png') or new texture per particle - breaks offline AND defeats ParticleContainer batching

guidelines.rule: Pool particle sprites once before the ticker and only TOGGLE/REPOSITION them - never create or destroy sprites per frame or per emission. Pre-build MAX sprites (all visible=false) and add them to the ParticleContainer up front; spawning grabs a hidden sprite and sets visible=true + position/tint/alpha; despawning sets visible=false back into the pool. Do NOT call pc.removeChild()/sprite.destroy() in the emission loop, and do NOT new PIXI.Sprite() on each spark.
guidelines.why: The fountain/swarm/blob autofix failures are exactly the symptom of create/destroy churn: allocating and destroying thousands of sprites per second triggers GC stalls and the GPU-resource destroy cost of pixi-007, dropping to a slideshow or stalling the autofixer mid-build. A fixed pool keeps allocation flat and the frame rate steady, and pairs with the property flags (pixi-015) and shared texture (pixi-016).
examples: spawn(): const s = pool[deadIndex++]; s.visible = true; s.x = ox; s.y = oy; s.alpha = 1; s.tint = 0xffd27f;
despawn(): s.visible = false; s.alpha = 0; // return to pool, do not destroy
WRONG: for each spark: const s = new PIXI.Sprite(tex); pc.addChild(s); ... later pc.removeChild(s); s.destroy(); - GC churn, autofix never converges

guidelines.rule: Handle resize by calling app.renderer.resize(width, height) (or rely on resizeTo: window) and then recompute layout-dependent constants and destroy+regenerate any size-dependent procedural textures. On resize: app.renderer.resize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight); re-derive emitter origin/bounds/wall positions from app.screen.width/height; if a texture was generated at the old size, call oldTexture.destroy(true) BEFORE assigning the newly generated one so VRAM does not leak.
guidelines.why: A fixed-size stage and stale size-dependent textures are why high-DPR/odd-size renders looked clipped or misaligned in the failing builds. app.renderer.resize is the correct v7 resize call; recomputing bounds from app.screen keeps gameplay/emitters correct after rotation; and destroying the old generated texture before regenerating (pixi-007) prevents the long-lived-canvas texture leak the failure notes flagged for resize handlers.
examples: window.addEventListener('resize', () => { app.renderer.resize(innerWidth, innerHeight); emitter.x = app.screen.width/2; repositionWalls(); });
regenerating a sized texture: const old = bgTex; bgTex = app.renderer.generateTexture(drawBg(app.screen.width, app.screen.height)); old.destroy(true);
WRONG: leaving the renderer at its initial fixed size, or generateTexture on resize without destroying the previous one
